comments.body: I know it´s late... but have you tried to remove the uc.setRequestProperty( "Accept", "text/xml" ); line??

Yes I know that I can use these clients.  However, I should be able to execute some variation of this code.  The problem is that it is failing at any line with, for example, connect(), getResponseCode, getContent, etc.  Basically it's not connecting and timing out.  But it works using Chrome or Firefox client.  If it works, I can run a script to do multiple POSTs required to create an account with valid properties, meters, etc. to generate metrics (which is what I'm testing).  Why is it not connecting?

answers.body:
You don't need to roll your own.

  • If you want to write something, you can use Jersey, which has existing classes to act as Rest Clients (Rest clients for Java?)
  • There are plenty of apps which exercise rest apis which you can use if you don't want to write something. Google turns up plenty (like http://code.google.com/p/rest-client/)
You're using a DER file as your key store which is not supported by Java Crypto normally. Use the keytool to create a JKS or some other supported keystore and then refer to it.

comments.body: Click on the link and scroll to basic... but of course i should copy it here. Sorry for that

answers.body:
AMong all the frameworks for REST-Clients... did you try OpenFeign? It's a components from the NetFlix stack. Easy to use and fits into all the other components of NetFlix.

Give it a try: https://github.com/OpenFeign/feign

tl;dr

In modern Java, use the built-in HTTP Client library to make the REST request manually using the provided authenticator feature for HTTP Basic Authentication. Or use purpose-built REST frameworks such as Jakarta RESTful Web Services.

HTTP Client

Java 11 brought a HTTP client supporting HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 per JEP 321. Work is underway to support HTTP/3 in Java 26 next year per JEP 517.

module-info.java

If modularized, add require java.net.http.

URI, not URL

The Java team now recommends using URI rather than URL class to parse or construct a URL.

URI uri = URI.create( "https://portfoliomanager.energystar.gov/wstest/account" );

Simple GET

You asked for:

code examples to do a simple GET

Here is some example code in Java 25+. (IO is the new System.out.)

Notice that we use try-with-resources to automatically close the java.net.http.HttpClient object.

package work.basil.example.web;

import java.io.IOException;
import java.net.URI;
import java.net.http.HttpClient;
import java.net.http.HttpRequest;
import java.net.http.HttpResponse;

public class ExHttpClientRest
{
    static void main ( )
    {
        System.out.println( Runtime.version() );
        try (
                HttpClient client = HttpClient.newHttpClient() )
        {
            URI uri = URI.create( "https://www.example.com" );
            // URI uri = URI.create( "https://portfoliomanager.energystar.gov/wstest/account" );
            HttpRequest request =
                    HttpRequest.newBuilder()
                            .uri( uri )
                            .build();

            HttpResponse < String > response = null;
            try
            {
                response = client.send(
                        request ,
                        HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofString()
                );
                String body = response.body();

                IO.println( "body:" );
                IO.println( body );
            }
            catch ( IOException e )
            {
                throw new RuntimeException( e );
            }
            catch ( InterruptedException e )
            {
                throw new RuntimeException( e );
            }
        }
    }
}

We receive the following HTML code as the response.

<!doctype html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>Example Domain</title>

    <meta charset="utf-8" />
    <meta http-equiv="Content-type" content="text/html; charset=utf-8" />
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
    <style type="text/css">
    body {
        background-color: #f0f0f2;
        margin: 0;
        padding: 0;
        font-family: -apple-system, system-ui, BlinkMacSystemFont, "Segoe UI", "Open Sans", "Helvetica Neue", Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif;
        
    }
    div {
        width: 600px;
        margin: 5em auto;
        padding: 2em;
        background-color: #fdfdff;
        border-radius: 0.5em;
        box-shadow: 2px 3px 7px 2px rgba(0,0,0,0.02);
    }
    a:link, a:visited {
        color: #38488f;
        text-decoration: none;
    }
    @media (max-width: 700px) {
        div {
            margin: 0 auto;
            width: auto;
        }
    }
    </style>    
</head>

<body>
<div>
    <h1>Example Domain</h1>
    <p>This domain is for use in illustrative examples in documents. You may use this
    domain in literature without prior coordination or asking for permission.</p>
    <p><a href="https://www.iana.org/domains/example">More information...</a></p>
</div>
</body>
</html>

Credentials

Of course, you do not have a simple GET. You have the complication of needing to pass login credentials. I do not know what you meant by “certificate” as that web site seems to be using simply HTTP Basic Authentication. (FYI, HTTP Basic Authentication is not real security.)

By the way, you said:

Without the certificate (commented out), the connection is timing out and throwing the exception at getResponseCode().

In my experience, without credentials I get immediately a 401 response, meaning Unauthorized, when using code seen above with your given URL.

You asked:

What is the correct way of submitting a certificate

Again, I do not know what you mean by “certificate”. But I can show you how to use HTTP Basic Authentication programmatically in Java.

The generative AI Claude.ai suggests using the HttpBin.org web site for practice while learning.

We can send a request with Basic Auth credentials "testuser" and "testpass" to https://httpbin.org/basic-auth/testuser/testpass. That URL returns JSON with your request details if auth succeeds, 401 if it fails.

Built-in authenticator feature

We can prompt a generative AI to write some code for us. Something like:

Write modern Java 25+ code showing how to send a request with HTTP Basic Authentication credentials "testuser" and "testpass" to the URL https://httpbin.org/basic-auth/testuser/testpass, and receive JSON in response.

From that AI you might learn the the Java HTTP Client has a built-in authenticator feature that we may call upon for our HTTP Basic Authentication. Just call HttpClient.Builder#authenticator. Pass a subclass of java.net.Authenticator while overriding getPasswordAuthentication().

Example code

After fixing the AI's errors, and massaging the code a bit, we get something like the following.

package work.basil.example.web;

import java.io.IOException;
import java.net.PasswordAuthentication;
import java.net.URI;
import java.net.http.HttpClient;
import java.net.http.HttpRequest;
import java.net.http.HttpResponse;
import java.time.Duration;

public class ExHttpClientRest
{
    static void main ( )
    {
        IO.println( Runtime.version() );

        // ----------|  HTTP Request  |-------------------------
        URI uri = URI.create( "https://httpbin.org/basic-auth/testuser/testpass" );
        HttpRequest request =
                HttpRequest.newBuilder()
                        .uri( uri )
                        .build();

        // ----------|  HTTP Client  |-------------------------
        try (
                HttpClient client =
                        HttpClient.newBuilder()
                                .connectTimeout( Duration.ofSeconds( 10 ) )
                                .authenticator(
                                        new java.net.Authenticator()
                                        {
                                            @Override
                                            protected PasswordAuthentication getPasswordAuthentication ( )
                                            {
                                                return new PasswordAuthentication( "testuser" , "testpass".toCharArray() );
                                            }
                                        }
                                )
                                .build() ;
        )
        {
            // ----------|  HTTP Response  |-------------------------
            HttpResponse < String > response = null;
            try
            {
                response = client.send(
                                request ,
                                HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofString()
                        );
                String body = response.body();

                IO.println( "body:" );
                IO.println( body );
            }
            catch ( IOException e )
            {
                throw new RuntimeException( e );
            }
            catch ( InterruptedException e )
            {
                throw new RuntimeException( e );
            }
        }
    }
}

When run, we get the following JSON:

{
  "authenticated": true, 
  "user": "testuser"
}

Explicit subclass of java.net.Authenticator

That call to .authenticator takes a subclass of java.net.Authenticator. We need to subclass so that we might override getPasswordAuthentication. To that override we pass an instance of java.net.PasswordAuthentication.

If you do not like the anonymous subclass, you could make an explicit subclass.

class SimpleAuthenticator extends Authenticator 
{
    private final String username;
    private final char[] password;

    SimpleAuthenticator( final String theUserName, final String thePassword ) 
    {
        this.username = theUserName;
        this.password = thePassword.toCharArray();
    }

    @Override
    protected PasswordAuthentication getPasswordAuthentication() 
    {
        return new PasswordAuthentication( this.username , this.password );
    }
}

Then pass an instance of that subclass.

.authenticator( new SimpleAuthenticator( "testuser", "testpass" ) )

REST frameworks: Jakarta EE, Spring, etc.

The code above is rather manual, getting your hands dirty in the HTTP request/response cycle. If you do much of this work, you will want to use the more automated features for REST calls provided by other frameworks. Those frameworks include Jakarta RESTful Web Services (multiple implementations), Spring, and others.

Jakarta RESTful Web Services

Let's try using the REST Client API defined in Jakarta RESTful Web Services.

We need the API defined by Jakarta EE. And we need an implementation of that API. Here we arbitrarily chose RESTEasy as our implementation. Adding dependencies in Maven POM:

<!-- Jakarta RESTful Web Services (JAX-RS) Client API -->

<!-- https://mvnrepository.com/artifact/jakarta.ws.rs/jakarta.ws.rs-api -->
<dependency>
    <groupId>jakarta.ws.rs</groupId>
    <artifactId>jakarta.ws.rs-api</artifactId>
    <version>4.0.0</version>
</dependency>

<!-- Add an implementation like RESTEasy Client -->
<!-- https://RESTEasy.dev/-->
<!-- https://mvnrepository.com/artifact/org.jboss.resteasy/resteasy-client -->
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.jboss.resteasy</groupId>
    <artifactId>resteasy-client</artifactId>
    <version>7.0.0.Beta1</version>
</dependency>

The HTTP client workload is shouldered by an implementation of the jakarta.ws.rs.client.Client interface specified in Jakarta. The results are communicated via a jakarta.ws.rs.core.Response object obtained via jakarta.ws.rs.client.WebTarget.

Notice how both Client and Response are AutoCloseable. We can automatically close those open resources by positioning them in a try-with-resources.

try
        (
                Client client = ClientBuilder.newClient() ;
                // Make a GET request to httpbin.org/get
                Response response = client
                        .target( "https://httpbin.org/get" )
                        .request( MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON )
                        .get() ;
        )
{
    // Check if the request was successful
    if ( response.getStatus() == 200 )
    {
        // Read the JSON response as a String
        String jsonResponse = response.readEntity( String.class );
        IO.println( "Response JSON:" );
        IO.println( jsonResponse );
    }
    else
    {
        IO.println( "Request failed with status: " + response.getStatus() );
    }

}
catch ( Exception e )
{
    System.err.println( "Error making REST call: " + e.getMessage() );
}

When run we get some JSON data that represents an echo of our request.

{
  "args": {}, 
  "headers": {
    "Accept": "application/json", 
    "Host": "httpbin.org", 
    "User-Agent": "Apache-HttpClient/4.5.14 (Java/25)", 
    "X-Amzn-Trace-Id": "Root=1-68d21cf5-7ee140a5354b86c46dbb5eab"
  }, 
  "origin": "149.22.88.137", 
  "url": "https://httpbin.org/get"
}

With credentials

Lastly, we need to convey credentials (username & password) to access the edition of that REST service that is protected by HTTP Basic Authentication.

This code below differs from above in that we call .header to add a header to our request. This header conveys our authentication credentials (username & password).

// ----------|  Provide Credentials  |-------------------------
// Username & password
final String username = "testuser";
final String password = "testpass";
// Create Basic Authentication header
final String auth = username + ":" + password;
final String encodedAuth = Base64.getEncoder().encodeToString( auth.getBytes() );
final String authHeader = "Basic " + encodedAuth;

try
        (
                // ----------|  HTTP Client  |-------------------------
                Client client = ClientBuilder.newClient() ;
                // ----------|  HTTP Response  |-------------------------
                // Make a GET request to httpbin.org/get
                Response response = client
                        .target( "https://httpbin.org/basic-auth/testuser/testpass" )
                        .request( MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON )
                        .header( "Authorization" , authHeader )
                        .get() ;
        )
{
    // Check if the request was successful
    if ( response.getStatus() == 200 )
    {
        // Read the JSON response as a String
        String jsonResponse = response.readEntity( String.class );
        IO.println( "Response JSON:" );
        IO.println( jsonResponse );
    }
    else
    {
        IO.println( "Request failed with status: " + response.getStatus() );
    }

}
catch ( Exception e )
{
    System.err.println( "Error making REST call: " + e.getMessage() );
}

When run, we get the following JSON.

{
  "authenticated": true, 
  "user": "testuser"
}

Caveat

I am not an expert in these matters. I just hacked around to figure this out because of curiosity.

This code is not well-tested. This code may need further work for practical use in production. Use at your own risk.

title: How to connect to REST web service from Java application with login credentials?
body:
I have to test the EPA's Data Exchange Web Services. Since it is difficult to create 100 accounts, buildings, energy usage distributions, etc. I want to automate the process. I searched for code examples to do a simple GET. The best one I found was at http://pic.dhe.ibm.com/infocenter/tivihelp/v10r1/index.jsp?topic=%2Fcom.ibm.taddm.doc_7.2%2FSDKDevGuide%2Ft_cmdbsdk_restapi_java.html. I modified this for my purposes.

  1. With the certificate, it is throwing an error at that line
  2. Without the certificate (commented out), the connection is timing out and throwing the exception at getResponseCode().

I'm not sure:

  1. What is the correct way of submitting a certificate
  2. If I am sending the credentials correctly
  3. If my code is incomplete, and therefore, the application is unable to get the response code
  4. I should be using Eclipse EE (with Web Tools Platform) and create Project > Web Application, instead of Eclipse Juno (without WTP)

Thank you in advance.

    package Package1;

import java.io.*;
import java.util.*;
import java.lang.StringBuffer;
import java.net.*;
import java.net.HttpURLConnection;
import javax.net.ssl.HttpsURLConnection;

public class Class1 {


    public static void main (String args[]){

        try{            

        // set this property to the location of the cert file
         System.setProperty("javax.net.ssl.trustStore","C:/Documents and Settings/bhattdr/Desktop/-.energystar.gov.der");   


        String username = "yy777PPP";
        String password = "yy777PPP";
        String userpass = "";

        URL url = new URL("https://portfoliomanager.energystar.gov/wstest/account");
//      URLConnection uc = url.openConnection();
        HttpsURLConnection uc = (HttpsURLConnection) url.openConnection();

        userpass = username + ":" + password;
        String basicAuth = "Basic " + javax.xml.bind.DatatypeConverter.printBase64Binary(userpass.getBytes());

        System.out.println("sending request...");

        uc.setRequestMethod("GET");
        uc.setAllowUserInteraction(false);
        uc.setDoOutput(true);
        uc.setRequestProperty( "Content-type", "text/xml" );
        uc.setRequestProperty( "Accept", "text/xml" );

        uc.setRequestProperty ("Authorization", basicAuth);

        System.out.println(uc.getRequestProperties());


//        uc.setRequestProperty( "authorization", "Basic " + encode("administrator:collation"));

//        Map headerFields = uc.getHeaderFields();
//        System.out.println("header fields are: " + headerFields);


        int rspCode = uc.getResponseCode();

        if (rspCode == 200) {
            InputStream is = uc.getInputStream();
            InputStreamReader isr = new InputStreamReader(is);
            BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(isr);

            String nextLine = br.readLine();
            while (nextLine != null) {
                System.out.println(nextLine);
                nextLine = br.readLine();
            }

        }
        }

        catch(IOException e) { 
            e.printStackTrace();
        }

    }
}
